content.problemStatement:
Chef has discovered a row of nnn balloons, each painted with a positive integer. Chef wants to burst all these balloons one by one.

When Chef bursts the ithi^{th}ith balloon, he earns coins equal to the product of the numbers on the adjacent balloons at that moment.
Coins=nums[left]×nums[i]×nums[right]Coins = nums[left] \times nums[i] \times nums[right]Coins=nums[left]×nums[i]×nums[right]


If there is no balloon on the left or right side (i.e., indices −1-1−1 or nnn), Chef treats it as if there is a balloon painted with **111**.
Chef aims to maximize the total coins collected by choosing the optimal order to burst the balloons. Help Chef find the maximum coins he can collect by bursting all the balloons wisely.

Function Declaration

**Function Name**



calculateMaxCoinscalculateMaxCoinscalculateMaxCoins



**Parameters**



balloonNumbersballoonNumbersballoonNumbers: A list of integers representing the numbers on each balloon.



**Return Value**



Returns an integerintegerinteger representing the maximum coins obtainable.


Constraints
1≤T≤1001 \le T \le 1001≤T≤100
1≤n≤3001 \le n \le 3001≤n≤300
0≤balloonNumbers[i]≤1000 \le balloonNumbers[i] \le 1000≤balloonNumbers[i]≤100
Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains an integer nnn — the number of balloons.
The second line contains nnn integers representing the numbers painted on each balloon.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer — the maximum coins Chef can collect.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T — the number of test cases. For each test case: The first line contains an integer n — the number of balloons. The second line contains n integers representing the numbers painted on each balloon.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer — the maximum coins Chef can collect.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1:
For the input 9 1 5 7, the optimal strategy to collect maximum coins is as follows:

Burst the balloon at index 1 (value 1): Coins = 9×1×5=45.
Burst the balloon at index 2 (value 5): Coins = 9×5×7=315.
Burst the balloon at index 3 (value 7): Coins = 9×7×1=63.
Finally, burst the balloon at index 0 (value 9): Coins = 1×9×1=9.
This sequence maximizes the product of adjacent values at each step, resulting in a total of 45+315+63+9=432 coins.

Test Case 2:
For the input 3 1 2 4 6, Chef must carefully choose the bursting order. By prioritizing balloons that maximize the product with their current neighbors, the optimal sequence yields a total of 126 coins.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we use dynamic programming. We create a 2D array dp where dp[left][right] represents the maximum coins we can get by bursting the balloons from left to right. We iterate over all possible lengths of the subarray and for each length, we try to burst each balloon and calculate the maximum coins we can get. We update dp[left][right] with the maximum coins we can get by bursting the balloons from left to right.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n^3) — where n is the number of balloons. This is because we have three nested loops: one for the length of the subarray, one for the left boundary, and one for the right boundary.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n^2) — where n is the number of balloons. This is because we need to store the maximum coins we can get for each subarray in the dp array.
